title: Past Tense
situation: Talk about what happened â€” yesterday, last week, your whole life story

phrases.context: Simple past: verb root + a/i/e. 'gaya' (male), 'gayi' (female). NO helper verb needed for 'I'
'aana' (to come) becomes 'aaya/aayi'. Pattern: verb stem + aa sound + ya/yi for gender
When the verb is transitive (has an object), subject gets 'ne': humâ†’humne, mainâ†’maine, usne (he/she)

phrases.hindi: maine kaha tha / maine kahi thi
phrases.english: I had said (to a male topic) / I had said (to a female topic)
phrases.context: 'tha/thi' after past verb = past perfect (had done). Gender matches OBJECT with transitive verbs!
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN-ne KA-ha tha / MAIN-ne KA-hi thi

phrases.hindi: tum kahan the? / tum kahan thi?
phrases.english: Where were you? (to male) / Where were you? (to female)
phrases.context: 'tha/thi/the' alone = was/were. 'the' for male plural/tum-male, 'thi' for female. Very useful!
phrases.pronunciation: TUM ka-HAAN THE? / TUM ka-HAAN THI?

phrases.context: 'usne' = he/she + ne (past transitive marker). 'mujhe' = to me. 'phone karna' = to call

phrases.english: Yesterday I didn't go to office
phrases.context: 'kal' = yesterday/tomorrow (context decides). 'nahi' before verb = didn't. Simple negation!

phrases.hindi: bachhpan mein hum roz khelke aate the
phrases.english: In childhood, we used to come back after playing daily
phrases.context: Past habitual: verb + te/ti + the/thi/tha. 'aate the' = used to come. Expresses old habits
phrases.pronunciation: BACH-pan mein HUM ROZ khel-TE aa-TE THE

phrases.context: 'hua' = happened (past of 'hona' = to be/happen). One of the most common past tense phrases

phrases.hindi: maine socha ki tum aa rahe ho
phrases.english: I thought that you were coming
phrases.context: 'socha' = thought (past of 'sochna'). 'ki' = that (conjunction). Combining tenses in one sentence
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN-ne SO-cha ki TUM AA ra-HE ho

grammar_notes: Simple past: verb root + a (male sg), i (female sg), e (male plural). Example: kha-ya (ate-male), kha-yi (ate-female), kha-ye (ate-plural male)
The 'ne' rule: With transitive verbs (verbs that have an object), the subject takes 'ne' â€” maine, tune, usne, humne, unhone. The verb then agrees with the OBJECT's gender, not the subject!
Was/were: tha (male sg), thi (female sg), the (male plural/tum), theen (female plural). 'Main tha' = I was (male), 'Main thi' = I was (female)
Past habitual (used to): verb-ta/ti/te + tha/thi/the. 'Main jaata tha' = I used to go. Same as present tense form but with tha/thi/the instead of hoon/hai/ho
Common irregular past forms: jaanaâ†’gaya, aanaâ†’aaya, karnaâ†’kiya, denaâ†’diya, lenaâ†’liya, honaâ†’hua
culture_notes: 'Kal' means both yesterday AND tomorrow â€” you figure out which from context. If ambiguous, add 'beeta kal' (past yesterday) or 'aane wala kal' (coming tomorrow)
Indians often use past tense to be polite: 'maine socha ki...' (I thought that...) instead of directly stating what you want
practice_prompt: Practice past tense by telling me about your day, what you did last weekend, or asking me what happened. I'll help you with the -a/-i/-e endings and the tricky 'ne' rule.
